title: What Is Rheumatoid Arthritis?
summary: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune disease that causes joint inflammation, pain, and damage. Understanding it is the first step toward living well.
body:
Rheumatoid arthritis — often called RA — is a chronic autoimmune disease. Unlike osteoarthritis, which is caused by everyday wear and tear, RA occurs when your immune system mistakenly attacks the lining of your own joints, called the synovium. This causes painful inflammation that can lead to joint swelling, stiffness, and, over time, joint damage.

RA most commonly affects the small joints of the hands and feet, but it can also involve larger joints like the knees, hips, elbows, and shoulders. It tends to be symmetric, meaning it usually affects the same joints on both sides of your body — both wrists, both knees, and so on.

The hallmark symptoms of RA include joint pain and tenderness, morning stiffness that lasts more than 30 minutes, swelling and warmth in affected joints, and fatigue. Fatigue is one of the most underappreciated symptoms. Many patients describe it as a bone-deep exhaustion that is not relieved by rest.

RA is more than a joint disease. Because it is systemic — affecting the whole body — it can also involve other organs, including the lungs, heart, and eyes. This is why seeing a rheumatologist regularly, even when you feel well, is so important.

Diagnosis is based on your symptoms, a physical exam, blood tests (including rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies), and imaging such as X-rays or ultrasound. Not everyone with RA has abnormal blood tests, so your doctor interprets all of this together.

The good news: RA is one of the most treatable autoimmune conditions. With the right medication, most patients can achieve low disease activity or even remission — a state where the disease is well controlled. Early, aggressive treatment leads to the best long-term outcomes.

Treatment usually starts with disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs), most commonly methotrexate. If that is not enough on its own, biologic medications that target specific parts of the immune system are added. Your rheumatologist tailors treatment to you.

Living well with RA also involves protecting your joints, staying active with appropriate exercise, managing stress, and attending all of your follow-up visits. The partnership between you and your rheumatology team is one of the most important factors in your outcome.

title: Understanding Your Lupus Labs
summary: Lab tests are central to diagnosing and monitoring lupus. Here is a plain-language guide to the most common ones and what they mean for you.
body:
If you have systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), also called lupus, you have probably had many blood and urine tests. Lab work is how your rheumatologist confirms the diagnosis, monitors disease activity, watches for organ involvement, and checks that your medications are safe. Understanding what these tests measure helps you be a more engaged partner in your care.

ANA (Antinuclear Antibody): This is almost always the first test ordered when lupus is suspected. A positive ANA simply means your immune system is making antibodies against certain proteins inside cells. It is present in over 95% of lupus patients, but it is not specific to lupus — many healthy people and people with other conditions also have a positive ANA. On its own, a positive ANA does not mean you have lupus.

Anti-dsDNA and Anti-Sm: These are more specific to lupus. Anti-dsDNA (antibody against double-stranded DNA) is important because its levels often rise when lupus is flaring and fall when disease is controlled. Anti-Sm is very specific to lupus and, when positive, strongly supports the diagnosis.

Complement levels (C3 and C4): Complement proteins are part of your immune system. In lupus, complement levels often drop during a flare because the immune complexes driving inflammation consume them. Low C3 or C4, especially combined with a rising anti-dsDNA, can be an early warning sign that your disease is becoming more active.

CBC (Complete Blood Count): Lupus frequently affects blood cells. Your doctor monitors your white cells (infection fighters), red cells (oxygen carriers), and platelets (clotters). Low counts in any of these can reflect disease activity, medication side effects, or both.

Urinalysis and urine protein: Lupus nephritis — lupus involvement of the kidneys — is one of the most serious complications. A simple urine dipstick and a urine protein-to-creatinine ratio can detect it early, often before you feel any symptoms. This is why every lupus clinic visit includes a urine check.

CrP and ESR: These are general markers of inflammation. They can be elevated in lupus flares, though CRP is sometimes normal even in active lupus (and tends to rise more with infections), while ESR is more consistently elevated.

Kidney function tests (creatinine, BUN): These check how well your kidneys are filtering waste from your blood. Your rheumatologist compares these to your baseline over time.

CBC and metabolic panels are also used to monitor medication safety — methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, mycophenolate, and other lupus drugs all require periodic monitoring.

One final note: no single test tells the whole story in lupus. Your rheumatologist interprets your labs together with your symptoms and examination findings. Never adjust your medications based on lab results alone without speaking with your doctor.

title: Managing Gout Flares: What to Do When the Pain Hits
summary: Gout flares can be sudden and excruciating. Knowing what to do in the first hours — and what to avoid — makes a real difference.
body:
Gout is one of the most painful forms of arthritis. Flares come on suddenly, often in the middle of the night, and the pain can be severe enough to make even the weight of a bed sheet unbearable. The good news is that gout is highly treatable, and with the right management, many patients go years or even a lifetime without another flare.

What causes a gout flare? Gout occurs when uric acid builds up in the blood (hyperuricemia) and crystallizes inside joints. The crystals trigger an intense inflammatory response. The big toe is the classic location — this is called podagra — but gout can also strike the ankle, knee, wrist, or elbow.

What to do when a flare starts:

Start treatment early. The sooner you treat, the faster the flare resolves. If your rheumatologist has given you a flare kit (colchicine, NSAIDs, or prednisone), begin it at the first signs of a flare.

Colchicine: This is usually the preferred first-line treatment for an acute flare. Low-dose colchicine — 1.2 mg at onset, then 0.6 mg one hour later — is highly effective and has fewer side effects than older higher-dose regimens. Take it within 12-24 hours of flare onset for best results.

NSAIDs (like indomethacin, naproxen, or ibuprofen): These anti-inflammatory pain relievers work well for gout flares. Take them with food. Avoid NSAIDs if you have kidney disease, a history of stomach ulcers, or heart failure — discuss with your doctor before your next flare.

Prednisone (corticosteroids): When colchicine and NSAIDs are not safe or not working, a short course of prednisone can rapidly calm a flare.

Rest the joint. Elevate and ice the affected area (never heat — heat worsens the inflammatory response).

Stay well hydrated. Drinking plenty of water helps your kidneys clear uric acid.

What NOT to do during a flare: Do not start or stop uric acid-lowering therapy (like allopurinol or febuxostat) during an active flare. This can prolong or worsen the attack. Continue any uric acid-lowering medicine you are already taking, but do not make changes.

After the flare: The goal is to keep your blood uric acid level below 6 mg/dL (or below 5 mg/dL if you have tophi or frequent flares). This usually requires uric acid-lowering medication taken daily, long term. Diet helps, but medication is usually essential. Together with your rheumatologist, you can build a plan to prevent the next flare.

title: Methotrexate: What to Expect
summary: Methotrexate is one of the most widely used and effective treatments for RA and other autoimmune conditions. Here is a guide to what to expect when starting it.
body:
Methotrexate (MTX) has been used to treat rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune conditions for decades. It remains the cornerstone of RA treatment — often the first disease-modifying drug prescribed — and when taken correctly with proper monitoring, it is remarkably safe and effective for most patients.

How it works: Methotrexate reduces joint inflammation by suppressing certain parts of your overactive immune system. It does not eliminate the immune system — it modulates it. At the low weekly doses used in rheumatology (typically 10 to 25 mg per week), it is very different from the high doses used in cancer chemotherapy.

Dosing: Methotrexate for RA is taken once a week, not daily. This is important — taking it more frequently greatly increases the risk of serious side effects. Your dose is usually started low (7.5-10 mg) and increased gradually based on your response and tolerability.

Folic acid is essential: Your rheumatologist will prescribe folic acid (vitamin B9) to take alongside methotrexate, usually 1 mg daily on the days you do not take methotrexate (some doctors prescribe 5 mg once a week). Folic acid dramatically reduces the most common side effects — nausea, mouth sores, and hair thinning — without reducing how well methotrexate works.

Common side effects: Nausea and fatigue are most common, usually occurring in the 24-48 hours after your dose. Taking your dose at night, taking it with food, switching to injectable methotrexate (more is absorbed, same dose = less gut exposure), and ensuring you take folic acid all help.

Mouth sores (oral ulcers) are another common side effect, also mitigated by folic acid.

Hair thinning can occur but is usually mild and reversible.

Rarer but serious side effects: Liver toxicity is possible with long-term use, which is why your rheumatologist monitors your liver enzymes with regular blood tests. Avoid more than 1-2 alcoholic drinks per week while on methotrexate — or none at all, ideally — as alcohol multiplies the liver risk.

Lung toxicity is rare but can occur. Report any new cough or shortness of breath to your doctor promptly.

Because methotrexate suppresses the immune system, you may be more susceptible to infections. Stay up to date on vaccinations (live vaccines are generally avoided while on immunosuppressive therapy — ask your rheumatologist).

Pregnancy: Methotrexate is teratogenic (harmful to a developing baby) and must be stopped well before conception for both women and men. If you are planning a pregnancy, discuss this with your rheumatologist well in advance.

Monitoring: You will have blood tests (CBC, liver function, kidney function) periodically — typically every 1-3 months when starting and every 3-6 months once stable. Do not skip these — they are how your rheumatologist catches any problems before they become serious.

When does it start working? Methotrexate typically takes 6-12 weeks to show meaningful benefits, and its full effect may take 3-6 months. It is important not to give up too soon.

title: Why Biologics Are Not as Scary as They Sound
summary: Biologic medications revolutionized the treatment of autoimmune diseases, but they can sound intimidating. Here is what they actually are and why they often work when other treatments have not.
body:
When your rheumatologist first mentions biologics, your reaction might be concern. These drugs come with long lists of side effects, they are given by injection or infusion, and their cost without insurance can be staggering. But for many patients with rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, psoriatic arthritis, and other autoimmune conditions, biologics represent the difference between ongoing joint damage and true disease control. Understanding what they are makes them much less intimidating.

What is a biologic? Biologics are medications made from living biological sources — typically proteins grown in cell cultures. Rather than suppressing the immune system broadly (like traditional DMARDs such as methotrexate), biologics target very specific molecules that drive inflammation. Think of them as precision tools rather than a sledgehammer.

The most common biologics for RA and related conditions target:

TNF (tumor necrosis factor): TNF-alpha is a key inflammatory protein. TNF inhibitors like etanercept (Enbrel), adalimumab (Humira), infliximab (Remicade), certolizumab (Cimzia), and golimumab (Simponi) block it directly.

IL-6: Tocilizumab (Actemra) and sarilumab (Kevzara) block the interleukin-6 pathway.

CD20 (B cells): Rituximab (Rituxan) depletes certain immune cells that contribute to autoimmune disease.

CTLA-4 (T cell activation): Abatacept (Orencia) interferes with T cell activation.

JAK inhibitors: While technically not biologics (they are small molecules), drugs like tofacitinib (Xeljanz), baricitinib (Olumiant), and upadacitinib (Rinvoq) work inside cells to block signaling pathways. They are often discussed alongside biologics.

Are biologics safe? All powerful medications have potential risks, and biologics are no exception. The main concerns are:

Infection risk: Biologics can lower your resistance to infections, including serious ones. Tuberculosis screening before starting a TNF inhibitor is routine. Report fevers, unusual infections, or respiratory symptoms promptly.

Vaccines: Live vaccines (like the MMR, live flu spray, or shingles live vaccine) are generally not given while on biologics. Inactivated vaccines (like flu shots, pneumonia, COVID-19, and the recombinant shingles vaccine Shingrix) are recommended and encouraged.

Skin cancers: Long-term use of biologics, particularly TNF inhibitors, is associated with a modestly increased risk of non-melanoma skin cancers. Use sunscreen and have annual skin checks.

The risks have to be weighed against the risk of uncontrolled RA — which includes progressive joint destruction, disability, and systemic complications.

How are biologics given? Most are given by self-injection under the skin, typically every 1-2 weeks or monthly, depending on the drug. Infliximab and abatacept are given intravenously. Injection devices are easy to use — similar to an epipen — and many patients are surprised by how quickly they become routine.

Cost and access: Biologics are expensive, but patient assistance programs, biosimilars (lower-cost biologic versions, similar to generics for traditional drugs), and insurance coverage have made them accessible for many patients. Your rheumatology team's practice manager or nurse can help navigate this.

Biologics are not a last resort. They are a logical next step when your disease is not adequately controlled. For many patients, they are the turning point toward a better quality of life.

title: Living Well with Psoriatic Arthritis
summary: Psoriatic arthritis affects joints and skin. Managing it well requires addressing both — and paying attention to the surprising ways the disease can affect overall health.
body:
Psoriatic arthritis (PsA) is an inflammatory arthritis that occurs in some people with psoriasis, the skin condition that causes red, scaly patches. About 1 in 3 people with psoriasis develop psoriatic arthritis, though in some cases the joint disease comes before any obvious skin disease.

What makes PsA unique: Unlike rheumatoid arthritis, PsA is often asymmetric — it may affect a joint on one side but not the other. It frequently involves the spine and sacroiliac joints (causing back pain and stiffness), the tendons and ligaments (enthesitis — inflammation where tendons attach to bone), and the fingers and toes (dactylitis — a puffy, sausage-like swelling of an entire digit).

PsA also has several distinct patterns. Some patients predominantly have peripheral joint disease; others have spine involvement; others have mainly skin disease. This variability means treatment has to be tailored to your specific manifestations.

The skin and joints are linked: Psoriasis and PsA share the same underlying immune mechanisms. Treating the immune driver — with DMARDs or biologics — often improves both the skin and the joints simultaneously. A drug that works well for one aspect of your disease is more likely to help the other.

Cardiovascular and metabolic health: PsA is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, hypertension, obesity, and metabolic syndrome. Controlling inflammation is protective, but lifestyle factors matter enormously. Achieving and maintaining a healthy weight, staying active, not smoking, and managing blood pressure and cholesterol are all parts of comprehensive PsA management.

Treatment options:

NSAIDs: For mild joint disease or enthesitis, NSAIDs can provide significant relief.

Traditional DMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine, leflunomide): These are used for peripheral joint disease, though they are less effective for spine involvement or enthesitis.

Biologics: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab, golimumab), IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab), and IL-12/23 or IL-23 inhibitors (ustekinumab, guselkumab, risankizumab) are all FDA-approved for PsA. The choice depends on your disease pattern, other health conditions, and preferences.

JAK inhibitors: Tofacitinib and upadacitinib are also approved for PsA.

Exercise and physical therapy: Low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling, walking) maintains joint function and reduces stiffness. Physical and occupational therapy can teach you to protect your joints.

Skin care: Dermatology and rheumatology often work together for PsA patients. Do not neglect your skin — plaque psoriasis that is well controlled may indicate your systemic treatment is working.

Regular monitoring: PsA can damage joints silently, and changes in your disease pattern are common over time. Regular visits to your rheumatologist — even when you feel well — allow for treatment adjustments before significant damage occurs.

title: Osteoarthritis vs. Inflammatory Arthritis: What Is the Difference?
summary: Many patients are confused about whether they have osteoarthritis or an inflammatory arthritis like RA. The distinction matters because the treatments are very different.
body:
Arthritis is not one disease — it is a term for over 100 different conditions that affect the joints. The two broadest categories are osteoarthritis (OA) and inflammatory arthritis (which includes rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, gout, and lupus arthritis). Understanding the difference helps explain why the same symptom — joint pain — can require entirely different treatments.

Osteoarthritis: wear and tear

OA is the most common form of arthritis. It occurs when the cartilage that cushions the ends of your bones gradually breaks down, causing bone to rub against bone. OA is driven primarily by mechanical factors — age, prior joint injury, obesity, repetitive joint stress, and genetics.

Key features of OA:
- Pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest
- Stiffness that lasts less than 30 minutes in the morning
- Involvement of weight-bearing joints (knees, hips, spine) and the base-of-thumb joint and the end knuckles of the fingers (DIP joints)
- Bony enlargement of joints (Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes in the fingers)
- Generally asymmetric (one knee may be much worse than the other)
- X-rays show joint space narrowing, bone spurs, and sclerosis
- Blood markers of inflammation (CRP, ESR) are usually normal

Treatment of OA focuses on pain management (acetaminophen, topical NSAIDs, oral NSAIDs, intra-articular injections), weight loss if applicable, physical therapy and strengthening, and — in advanced cases — joint replacement surgery.

Inflammatory arthritis: immune system gone awry

Inflammatory arthritis occurs when the immune system attacks the joints (and sometimes other organs), causing chronic inflammation.

Key features of inflammatory arthritis:
- Morning stiffness lasting more than 30-60 minutes ("gelling" after rest)
- Joint swelling, warmth, and redness driven by inflammation
- Symmetric involvement (both wrists, both MCPs in RA)
- Systemic symptoms: fatigue, low-grade fever
- Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR)
- Abnormal autoantibodies (rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP in RA; ANA in lupus)
- X-rays may show erosions (bone destruction) in RA

Treatment of inflammatory arthritis targets the immune driver — DMARDs, biologics — to prevent joint damage, not just relieve pain.

Why does it matter? Treating inflammatory arthritis with only pain relievers (the approach for OA) leaves the underlying immune activity unchecked, leading to progressive joint and organ damage. Conversely, treating OA with immunosuppressants has risks without benefits. Your rheumatologist's job is to make the correct diagnosis so the right treatment can begin.

Can someone have both? Yes. Many people — particularly older adults — have OA in some joints and an inflammatory arthritis in others. This is another reason careful evaluation is essential.

title: What Is Hydroxychloroquine and Why Is It So Commonly Used?
summary: Hydroxychloroquine (Plaquenil) is prescribed for lupus, early RA, and other autoimmune conditions. It is one of the most protective medications in rheumatology — here is why.
body:
Hydroxychloroquine — brand name Plaquenil — is one of the most commonly prescribed medications in rheumatology. Originally developed as an antimalarial drug, it was discovered decades ago to have powerful immune-modulating effects that make it invaluable in lupus and rheumatoid arthritis. If you have been prescribed it, here is what you should know.

How does it work? Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ) works differently from most immunosuppressants. Rather than broadly suppressing the immune system, it interferes with the activation of certain immune cells and with the signaling pathways that drive autoimmune inflammation. It is absorbed into cells and disrupts the acidic compartments (lysosomes) that immune cells use to process inflammatory signals. The full mechanism is still being studied, but the clinical effects are well established.

Why is it used in lupus?

HCQ is considered a cornerstone of lupus treatment. Studies show it:
- Reduces lupus flares
- Decreases the risk of organ damage, particularly kidney and neurological involvement
- Reduces the risk of blood clots (thrombosis), which are a major concern in lupus
- May reduce mortality
- Protects against disease progression during pregnancy
- Has anti-inflammatory effects on the skin, joints, and fatigue

Because of this evidence, guidelines recommend that virtually every lupus patient who can tolerate it should be on hydroxychloroquine — not just those with active disease.

Why is it used in early RA?

In early or mild rheumatoid arthritis, HCQ is sometimes used — often as part of a combination regimen (the "triple therapy" of methotrexate + sulfasalazine + HCQ). It is less potent than methotrexate alone for most patients, but it is very well tolerated and can add benefit when used in combination.

Dose: The dose used in rheumatology is 200-400 mg per day (for adults). Current guidelines recommend dosing based on body weight (typically 5 mg/kg of actual body weight) to minimize the main long-term risk: retinal toxicity.

Retinal toxicity: The main serious concern with long-term HCQ use is a risk to the retina (the light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye). At standard doses, this risk is very low in the first 5 years of use but increases with higher doses and longer duration. For this reason, annual eye exams with a specific screening test (the 10-2 visual field and OCT imaging) are recommended after 5 years of use — or sooner if you have risk factors (kidney disease, macular disease, very high doses). Do not skip your eye exams.

Common and uncommon side effects: GI upset (nausea, stomach cramping) is the most common side effect and is usually avoided by taking HCQ with food. A change in skin pigmentation (bluish-grey discoloration, particularly on the shins and face) is uncommon but reversible over time if the drug is stopped. Cardiomyopathy (heart muscle disease) is very rare but is checked for in long-term patients.

When does it start working? HCQ is not a fast-acting drug. It can take 2-3 months to see benefit, and the full effect on lupus activity may take 6 months. This is normal — do not stop it because it is not working immediately.

HCQ is one of the safest long-term medications in rheumatology. With proper monitoring, most patients take it for years or even decades without significant problems.
